  1268	#[tokio::test(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 4)]
  1269	async fn payload_does_not_wait_for_shape_ramp_under_either_initiator() {
  1270	    // 2,000 one-byte files produce shape target 4. Gate ACK #2 (the 2→3
  1271	    // epoch), require ALL payloads to finish sending while it remains held,
  1272	    // then release it and require the same exact final target under both
  1273	    // connection-role layouts. This deterministically guards first-byte /
  1274	    // useful-work progress without relying on wall-clock RTT assumptions.
  1275	    const FILE_COUNT: usize = 2_000;
  1276	    const TARGET_STREAMS: usize = 4;
  1277
  1278	    for initiator_role in [TransferRole::Source, TransferRole::Destination] {
  1279	        let tmp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
  1280	        let src_root = tmp.path().join("src");
  1281	        let dst_root = tmp.path().join("dst");
  1282	        std::fs::create_dir_all(&src_root).unwrap();
  1283	        std::fs::create_dir_all(&dst_root).unwrap();
  1284	        for i in 0..FILE_COUNT {
  1285	            std::fs::write(src_root.join(format!("f{i:05}.bin")), b"x").unwrap();
  1286	        }
  1287
  1288	        let open = SessionOpen {
  1289	            initiator_role: initiator_role as i32,
  1290	            compare_mode: ComparisonMode::SizeMtime as i32,
  1291	            in_stream_bytes: false,
  1292	            ..Default::default()
  1293	        };
  1294	        let (source_endpoint, dest_endpoint, source_host, dest_host) = match initiator_role {
  1295	            TransferRole::Source => (
  1296	                SessionEndpoint::initiator(open),
  1297	                SessionEndpoint::Responder,
  1298	                Some("127.0.0.1".into()),
  1299	                None,
  1300	            ),
  1301	            TransferRole::Destination => (
  1302	                SessionEndpoint::Responder,
  1303	                SessionEndpoint::initiator(open),
  1304	                None,
  1305	                Some("127.0.0.1".into()),
  1306	            ),
  1307	            TransferRole::Unspecified => unreachable!(),
  1308	        };
  1309
  1310	        let (progress_tx, mut progress_rx) = tokio::sync::mpsc::unbounded_channel();
  1311	        let source_cfg = SourceSessionConfig {
  1312	            instruments: SourceInstruments {
  1313	                progress: Some(RemoteTransferProgress::new(progress_tx)),
  1314	                ..Default::default()
  1315	            },
  1316	            hello: HelloConfig::default(),
  1317	            endpoint: source_endpoint,
  1318	            plan_options: PlanOptions::default(),
  1319	            data_plane_host: source_host,
  1320	        };
  1321	        let dest_cfg = DestinationSessionConfig {
  1322	            hello: HelloConfig::default(),
  1323	            endpoint: dest_endpoint,
  1324	            data_plane_host: dest_host,
  1325	            instruments: Default::default(),
  1326	            local_apply: None,
  1327	        };
  1328
  1329	        let seen = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
  1330	        let delivered = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
  1331	        let waiting = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));
  1332	        let reached = Arc::new(tokio::sync::Notify::new());
  1333	        let release = Arc::new(tokio::sync::Notify::new());
  1334	        let (source_transport, dest_transport) = in_process_pair();
  1335	        let (source_tx, source_rx) = source_transport.split();
  1336	        let source_transport = FrameTransport::new(
  1337	            source_tx,
  1338	            Box::new(GateNthResizeAckRx {
  1339	                inner: source_rx,
  1340	                gate_at: 2,
  1341	                seen: Arc::clone(&seen),
  1342	                delivered: Arc::clone(&delivered),
  1343	                waiting: Arc::clone(&waiting),
  1344	                reached: Arc::clone(&reached),
  1345	                release: Arc::clone(&release),
  1346	            }),
  1347	        );
  1348
  1349	        let source = Arc::new(FsTransferSource::new(src_root.clone()));
  1350	        let session = tokio::spawn({
  1351	            let dst_root = dst_root.clone();
  1352	            async move {
  1353	                tokio::join!(
  1354	                    run_source(source_cfg, source_transport, source),
  1355	                    run_destination(dest_cfg, dest_transport, DestinationTarget::Fixed(dst_root)),
  1356	                )
  1357	            }
  1358	        });
  1359
  1360	        let proof = tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(10), async {
  1361	            let payload_complete = async {
  1362	                let mut files = 0usize;
  1363	                while files < FILE_COUNT {
  1364	                    match progress_rx.recv().await.expect("progress lane stays open") {
  1365	                        ProgressEvent::FileComplete { .. } => files += 1,
  1366	                        ProgressEvent::ManifestBatch { .. } | ProgressEvent::Payload { .. } => {}
  1367	                    }
  1368	                }
  1369	            };
  1370	            tokio::join!(payload_complete, reached.notified());
  1371	        })
  1372	        .await;
  1373	        if proof.is_err() {
  1374	            release.notify_one();
  1375	            let _ = session.await;
  1376	            panic!("payload stalled behind resize ACK #2 with initiator {initiator_role:?}");
  1377	        }
  1378	        assert!(waiting.load(Ordering::SeqCst), "ACK #2 is held");
  1379	        assert_eq!(seen.load(Ordering::SeqCst), 2, "exactly two ACKs seen");
  1380	        assert_eq!(
  1381	            delivered.load(Ordering::SeqCst),
  1382	            1,
  1383	            "all payload completed before gated ACK #2 reached the SOURCE"
  1384	        );
  1385
  1386	        release.notify_one();
  1387	        let (source_result, dest_result) = tokio::time::timeout(SUITE_TIMEOUT, session)
  1388	            .await
  1389	            .expect("session run timed out")
  1390	            .expect("session task panicked");
  1391	        let summary = source_result.expect("source succeeds");
  1392	        let outcome = dest_result.expect("destination succeeds");
  1393	        assert_eq!(summary, outcome.summary);
  1394	        assert_eq!(summary.files_transferred, FILE_COUNT as u64);
  1395	        assert_eq!(outcome.data_plane_streams, Some(TARGET_STREAMS));
  1396	        assert_trees_identical(&src_root, &dst_root);
  1397	    }
  1398	}
  1399
  1400	#[tokio::test(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 4)]
  1401	async fn pull_data_plane_single_stream_lands_bytes() {
  1402	    // otp-5b-1: the transport/role decoupling in the PULL direction — the
  1403	    // mirror of the push data-plane test above. Here the DESTINATION is the
  1404	    // *initiator* (dials + receives) and the SOURCE is the *responder*
  1405	    // (binds + accepts + sends). Control frames ride the in-process
  1406	    // transport; the data-plane socket rides loopback TCP (the SOURCE
  1407	    // responder binds 0.0.0.0:0, the DESTINATION initiator dials
  1408	    // 127.0.0.1). Single-stream because this 4-file tree's shape wants only
  1409	    // one stream — the pull data plane CAN resize (otp-5b-2), but a small
  1410	    // need list never crosses the shape threshold; the resize itself is
  1479	#[tokio::test(flavor = "multi_thread", worker_threads = 4)]
  1480	async fn many_tiny_files_reach_shape_target_when_destination_initiates() {
  1481	    // otp-5b-2: the sf-2 shape correction in the PULL direction — the
  1482	    // mirror of `many_tiny_files_reach_shape_target_when_source_initiates`
  1483	    // (push). Here the DESTINATION is the *initiator* (dials the epoch-N
  1484	    // sockets it grows to) and the SOURCE is the *responder* (accepts them
  1485	    // off its bound listener). The control-lane `DataPlaneResize{ADD}` /
  1486	    // `DataPlaneResizeAck` frames are identical to push; only the transport
  1487	    // action flips. A 10k-tiny-file transfer must re-run the shape table
  1488	    // over the accumulated need list and grow the stream count past 1.
  1489	    let tmp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
  1490	    let src_root = tmp.path().join("src");
  1491	    let dst_root = tmp.path().join("dst");
  1492	    std::fs::create_dir_all(&src_root).unwrap();
  1493	    std::fs::create_dir_all(&dst_root).unwrap();
  1494	    const FILE_COUNT: usize = 10_000;
  1495	    for i in 0..FILE_COUNT {
  1496	        std::fs::write(src_root.join(format!("f{i:05}.bin")), b"x").unwrap();
  1497	    }
  1498
  1499	    // DESTINATION initiator; SOURCE responder — roles flipped from the push
  1500	    // shape test, the data plane following connection role.
  1501	    let open = SessionOpen {
  1502	        initiator_role: TransferRole::Destination as i32,
  1503	        compare_mode: ComparisonMode::SizeMtime as i32,
  1504	        in_stream_bytes: false,
  1505	        // Wire contract: zero means unknown, not a one-stream cap. Pin it
  1506	        // on the destination-initiator orientation, where this end both
  1507	        // advertises and enforces the receiver ceiling.
  1508	        receiver_capacity: Some(CapacityProfile {
  1509	            max_streams: 0,
  1510	            ..Default::default()
  1511	        }),
  1512	        ..Default::default()
  1513	    };
  1514	    let source_cfg = SourceSessionConfig {
  1515	        instruments: Default::default(),
  1516	        hello: HelloConfig::default(),
  1517	        endpoint: SessionEndpoint::Responder, // binds + accepts + sends
  1518	        plan_options: PlanOptions::default(),
  1519	        data_plane_host: None, // a responder never dials
  1520	    };
  1521	    let dest_cfg = DestinationSessionConfig {
  1522	        hello: HelloConfig::default(),
  1523	        endpoint: SessionEndpoint::initiator(open), // dials + receives
  1524	        data_plane_host: Some("127.0.0.1".into()),
  1525	        instruments: Default::default(),
  1526	        local_apply: None,
  1527	    };
  1528	    let (a, b) = in_process_pair();
  1529	    let source = Arc::new(FsTransferSource::new(src_root.clone()));
  1530	    let (source_result, dest_result) = tokio::time::timeout(SUITE_TIMEOUT, async {
  1531	        tokio::join!(
  1532	            run_source(source_cfg, a, source),
  1533	            run_destination(dest_cfg, b, DestinationTarget::Fixed(dst_root.clone())),
  1534	        )
  1535	    })
  1536	    .await
  1537	    .expect("session run timed out");
  1538
  1539	    let summary = source_result.expect("source responder succeeds");
  1540	    let outcome = dest_result.expect("destination initiator succeeds");
  1541	    assert!(
  1542	        !summary.in_stream_carrier_used,
  1543	        "the pull sf-2 pin must ride the TCP data plane"
  1544	    );
  1545	    assert_eq!(summary.files_transferred, FILE_COUNT as u64);
  1546	    let streams = outcome
  1547	        .data_plane_streams
  1548	        .expect("data plane ran, stream count recorded");
  1549	    assert_eq!(
  1550	        streams, 8,
  1551	        "a {FILE_COUNT}-file transfer must reach the shape policy's eight-stream \
  1552	         target regardless of which endpoint initiated the session"
  1553	    );
  1554	    assert_trees_identical(&src_root, &dst_root);
  1555	}
  1556
  1557	#[tokio::test]
  1558	async fn preserves_mtime_on_streamed_files() {
    50	/// [`DIAL_TUNER_TICK`]; below [`DIAL_STEP_UP_BLOCKED_RATIO`] blocked
    51	/// time the pipe is not back-pressured → step up; above
    52	/// [`DIAL_STEP_DOWN_BLOCKED_RATIO`] → step down. One step per tick
    53	/// (hysteresis by construction).
    54	pub const DIAL_TUNER_TICK: std::time::Duration = std::time::Duration::from_millis(500);
    55	pub const DIAL_STEP_UP_BLOCKED_RATIO: f64 = 0.05;
    56	pub const DIAL_STEP_DOWN_BLOCKED_RATIO: f64 = 0.30;
    57
    58	/// Resize policy (`ue-r2-2`): streams are the EXPENSIVE dial — a step
    59	/// costs a control round-trip plus a TCP connect — so they move only
    60	/// after the cheap dials are pinned at a bound and the signal has held
    61	/// for [`RESIZE_SUSTAIN_TICKS`] consecutive ticks, and never within
    62	/// [`RESIZE_COOLDOWN_TICKS`] of the previous settle. One stream per
    63	/// epoch (the wire carries one `sub_token` per ADD).
    64	pub const RESIZE_COOLDOWN_TICKS: u32 = 4;
    65	pub const RESIZE_SUSTAIN_TICKS: i32 = 2;
    66
    67	/// The capacity profile this host advertises when it is the byte
    68	/// RECEIVER (ue-r2-1e: the first real sender of the ue-r2-1b wire
    69	/// fields). Honest system facts only — fields we cannot measure yet
    70	/// stay 0 (= unknown per the wire contract), never fabricated:
    71	/// ceilings mirror what today's receive paths actually accept.
    72	pub fn local_receiver_capacity() -> CapacityProfile {
    73	    CapacityProfile {
    74	        cpu_cores: num_cpus::get() as u32,
    75	        drain_class: 0,
    76	        load_percent: 0,
    77	        max_streams: DIAL_CEILING_MAX_STREAMS as u32,
    78	        drain_rate_bytes_per_sec: 0,
    79	        max_chunk_bytes: DIAL_CEILING_CHUNK_BYTES as u64,
    80	        max_inflight_bytes: (DIAL_CEILING_CHUNK_BYTES * DIAL_CEILING_PREFETCH) as u64,
    81	    }
    82	}
    83
    84	/// Resolve the receiver's advertised stream ceiling with the wire
    85	/// contract's `0 = unknown` semantics. Both the SOURCE-owned dial and the
    86	/// DESTINATION's resize admission must call this one function; otherwise a
    87	/// destination-initiated session can interpret the same profile as a
    88	/// one-stream cap while its source interprets it as the default ceiling.
    89	pub fn receiver_stream_ceiling(profile: Option<&CapacityProfile>) -> usize {
    90	    profile
    91	        .and_then(|capacity| (capacity.max_streams > 0).then_some(capacity.max_streams as usize))
    92	        .unwrap_or(DIAL_CEILING_MAX_STREAMS)
    93	        .clamp(1, DIAL_CEILING_MAX_STREAMS)
    94	}
    95
    96	/// Serialized wire-epoch state. Resize proposals are rare (at most one per
    97	/// control-lane round trip), so one short critical section is preferable to
    98	/// a split-atomic check/CAS sequence that can reopen a refused transfer or
    99	/// reuse an epoch after an intervening settlement.
   100	#[derive(Debug, Default)]
   101	struct ResizeEpochState {
   102	    settled_epoch: u32,
   103	    pending_epoch: Option<u32>,
   104	    refused: bool,
   105	}
   106
   107	impl ResizeEpochState {
   108	    fn settle(&mut self, epoch: u32, accepted: bool) -> bool {
   109	        if self.pending_epoch != Some(epoch) || epoch == 0 {
   110	            return false;
   111	        }
   112	        if !accepted {
   113	            self.refused = true;
   114	        }
   115	        self.settled_epoch = epoch;
   116	        self.pending_epoch = None;
   117	        true
   118	    }
   119	}
   120
   121	struct ResizeEpochGuard<'a> {
   122	    inner: std::sync::MutexGuard<'a, ResizeEpochState>,
   123	    #[cfg(test)]
   124	    acquisition: usize,
   125	}
   126
   127	impl std::ops::Deref for ResizeEpochGuard<'_> {
   128	    type Target = ResizeEpochState;
   129
   130	    fn deref(&self) -> &Self::Target {
   131	        &self.inner
   132	    }
   133	}
   134
   135	impl std::ops::DerefMut for ResizeEpochGuard<'_> {
   136	    fn deref_mut(&mut self) -> &mut Self::Target {
   137	        &mut self.inner
   138	    }
   139	}
   140
   141	#[cfg(test)]
   142	impl ResizeEpochGuard<'_> {
   143	    fn acquisition(&self) -> usize {
   144	        self.acquisition
   145	    }
   146	}
   147
   148	#[cfg(test)]
   149	struct ResizeTickTestHook {
   150	    entered: std::sync::Barrier,
   151	    release: std::sync::Barrier,
   152	    entered_acquisition: AtomicUsize,
   153	    claimed_acquisition: AtomicUsize,
   154	}
   155
   156	#[cfg(test)]
   157	impl std::fmt::Debug for ResizeTickTestHook {
   158	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
   159	        f.write_str("ResizeTickTestHook")
   160	    }
   161	}
   162
   163	#[cfg(test)]
   164	impl ResizeTickTestHook {
   165	    fn new() -> Self {
   166	        Self {
   167	            entered: std::sync::Barrier::new(2),
   168	            release: std::sync::Barrier::new(2),
   169	            entered_acquisition: AtomicUsize::new(0),
   170	            claimed_acquisition: AtomicUsize::new(0),
   171	        }
   172	    }
   173	}
   174
   175	#[cfg(test)]
   176	struct ResizeSettleTestHook {
   177	    observed: std::sync::Barrier,
   178	    contended: std::sync::atomic::AtomicBool,
   179	}
   180
   181	#[cfg(test)]
   182	impl std::fmt::Debug for ResizeSettleTestHook {
   183	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
   184	        f.write_str("ResizeSettleTestHook")
   185	    }
   186	}
   187
   188	#[cfg(test)]
   189	impl ResizeSettleTestHook {
   190	    fn new() -> Self {
   300	            chunk_bytes: AtomicUsize::new(DIAL_FLOOR_CHUNK_BYTES.min(ceiling_chunk)),
   301	            prefetch_count: AtomicUsize::new(DIAL_FLOOR_PREFETCH.min(ceiling_prefetch)),
   302	            tcp_buffer_bytes: AtomicUsize::new(0),
   303	            initial_streams: AtomicUsize::new(DIAL_FLOOR_INITIAL_STREAMS.min(ceiling_streams)),
   304	            max_streams: AtomicUsize::new(DIAL_FLOOR_MAX_STREAMS.clamp(1, ceiling_streams.max(1))),
   305	            live_streams: AtomicUsize::new(DIAL_FLOOR_INITIAL_STREAMS.min(ceiling_streams)),
   306	            resize_epochs: Mutex::new(ResizeEpochState::default()),
   307	            #[cfg(test)]
   308	            resize_tick_test_hook: Mutex::new(None),
   309	            #[cfg(test)]
   310	            resize_settle_test_hook: Mutex::new(None),
   311	            #[cfg(test)]
   312	            resize_lock_sequence: AtomicUsize::new(0),
   313	            ticks_since_settle: AtomicU32::new(0),
   314	            resize_sustain: AtomicI32::new(0),
   315	            ceiling_chunk_bytes: ceiling_chunk,
   316	            ceiling_prefetch,
   317	            ceiling_max_streams: ceiling_streams,
   318	            ceiling_tcp_buffer_bytes: ceiling_tcp,
   319	        }
   320	    }
   321
   322	    pub fn shared(self) -> Arc<Self> {
   323	        Arc::new(self)
   324	    }
   325
   326	    // ── live reads ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
   327	    pub fn chunk_bytes(&self) -> usize {
   328	        self.chunk_bytes.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   329	    }
   330	    pub fn prefetch_count(&self) -> usize {
   331	        self.prefetch_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   332	    }
   333	    /// `None` = leave the kernel default (old `tcp_buffer_size`
   334	    /// semantics). Connect-time dial.
   335	    pub fn tcp_buffer_bytes(&self) -> Option<usize> {
   336	        match self.tcp_buffer_bytes.load(Ordering::Relaxed) {
   337	            0 => None,
   338	            n => Some(n),
   339	        }
   340	    }
   341	    pub fn initial_streams(&self) -> usize {
   342	        self.initial_streams.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   343	    }
   344	    /// Ceiling on the negotiated stream count (profile-clamped).
   345	    pub fn max_streams(&self) -> usize {
   346	        self.max_streams.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   347	    }
   348	    pub fn ceiling_max_streams(&self) -> usize {
   349	        self.ceiling_max_streams
   350	    }
   351
   352	    /// Record the stream count the negotiation actually settled on
   353	    /// (clamped to the dial's ceiling). This is the epoch-0 settle:
   354	    /// it also seeds `live_streams`, the baseline every `ue-r2-2`
   355	    /// resize proposal steps from.
   356	    pub fn set_negotiated_streams(&self, streams: usize) -> usize {
   357	        let clamped = streams.clamp(1, self.ceiling_max_streams.max(1));
   358	        self.initial_streams.store(clamped, Ordering::Relaxed);
   359	        self.live_streams.store(clamped, Ordering::Relaxed);
   360	        clamped
   361	    }
   362
   363	    // ── ue-r2-2 resize policy ────────────────────────────────────────
   364
   365	    /// The settled live stream count (epoch-0 negotiation, then each
   366	    /// accepted resize).
   367	    pub fn live_streams(&self) -> usize {
   368	        self.live_streams.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   369	    }
   370
   371	    /// Last settled resize epoch (0 = only the initial stream set).
   372	    pub fn resize_epoch(&self) -> u32 {
   373	        self.resize_epochs
   374	            .lock()
   375	            .expect("resize epoch state poisoned")
   376	            .settled_epoch
   377	    }
   378
   379	    /// True while a proposal is awaiting `resize_settled`.
   380	    pub fn resize_pending(&self) -> bool {
   381	        self.resize_epochs
   382	            .lock()
   383	            .expect("resize epoch state poisoned")
   384	            .pending_epoch
   385	            .is_some()
   386	    }
   387
   388	    fn cheap_dials_maxed(&self) -> bool {
   389	        self.chunk_bytes.load(Ordering::Relaxed) >= self.ceiling_chunk_bytes
   390	            && self.prefetch_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed) >= self.ceiling_prefetch
   391	    }
   392
   393	    fn cheap_dials_floored(&self) -> bool {
   394	        self.chunk_bytes.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   395	            <= DIAL_FLOOR_CHUNK_BYTES.min(self.ceiling_chunk_bytes)
   396	            && self.prefetch_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
   397	                <= DIAL_FLOOR_PREFETCH.min(self.ceiling_prefetch).max(1)
   398	    }
   399
   400	    /// One resize-eligible tuner tick. Streams move only as the LAST
   401	    /// escalation step in either direction: the cheap dials must
   402	    /// already be pinned at their ceiling (ADD) or floor (REMOVE), the
   403	    /// signal must hold for [`RESIZE_SUSTAIN_TICKS`] consecutive
   404	    /// ticks, at least [`RESIZE_COOLDOWN_TICKS`] must have passed
   405	    /// since the last settle, and no proposal may be in flight. Idle
   406	    /// ticks (`delta_bytes == 0`) are no signal, matching the cheap
   407	    /// tuner. Bounds: `1..=ceiling_max_streams` (the receiver profile
   408	    /// folded in at construction — `CapacityProfile.max_streams` is
   409	    /// authoritative per the proto). One stream per epoch.
   410	    ///
   411	    /// The caller must forward the returned proposal to the peer and
   412	    /// call [`Self::resize_settled`] with the outcome; until then
   413	    /// every subsequent tick returns `None`.
   414	    pub fn resize_tick(&self, delta_bytes: u64, blocked_ratio: f64) -> Option<ResizeProposal> {
   415	        // Keep eligibility, direction, live count, and epoch claim in the
   416	        // same critical section as settlement. Otherwise a shape resize can
   417	        // settle/reset cooldown between signal calculation and claim, and a
   418	        // stale tuner decision can immediately open the next epoch.
   419	        let mut state = self.lock_resize_epochs();
   420	        if state.refused || state.pending_epoch.is_some() {
   421	            return None;
   422	        }
   423	        #[cfg(test)]
   424	        let test_hook = self
   425	            .resize_tick_test_hook
   426	            .lock()
   427	            .expect("resize tick test hook poisoned")
   428	            .clone();
   429	        #[cfg(test)]
   430	        if let Some(hook) = test_hook.as_ref() {
   431	            hook.entered_acquisition
   432	                .store(state.acquisition(), Ordering::SeqCst);
   433	            hook.entered.wait();
   434	            hook.release.wait();
   435	        }
   436	        let ticks = self
   437	            .ticks_since_settle
   438	            .fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed)
   439	            .saturating_add(1);
   440	        if delta_bytes == 0 {
   441	            self.resize_sustain.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
   442	            return None;
   443	        }
   444	        let sustain = if blocked_ratio < DIAL_STEP_UP_BLOCKED_RATIO && self.cheap_dials_maxed() {
   445	            let prev = self.resize_sustain.load(Ordering::Relaxed).max(0);
   446	            let next = prev.saturating_add(1);
   447	            self.resize_sustain.store(next, Ordering::Relaxed);
   448	            next
   449	        } else if blocked_ratio > DIAL_STEP_DOWN_BLOCKED_RATIO && self.cheap_dials_floored() {
   450	            let prev = self.resize_sustain.load(Ordering::Relaxed).min(0);
   451	            let next = prev.saturating_sub(1);
   452	            self.resize_sustain.store(next, Ordering::Relaxed);
   453	            next
   454	        } else {
   455	            self.resize_sustain.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
   456	            0
   457	        };
   458	        if ticks < RESIZE_COOLDOWN_TICKS {
   459	            return None;
   460	        }
   461	        let add = if sustain >= RESIZE_SUSTAIN_TICKS {
   462	            true
   463	        } else if sustain <= -RESIZE_SUSTAIN_TICKS {
   464	            false
   465	        } else {
   466	            return None;
   467	        };
   468	        let live = self.live_streams.load(Ordering::Relaxed).max(1);
   469	        let target = if add {
   470	            (live + 1).min(self.ceiling_max_streams.max(1))
   471	        } else {
   472	            live.saturating_sub(1).max(1)
   473	        };
   474	        if target == live {
   475	            // Already at the bound in the wanted direction.
   476	            self.resize_sustain.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
   477	            return None;
   478	        }
   479	        let epoch = state.settled_epoch.checked_add(1)?;
   480	        state.pending_epoch = Some(epoch);
   481	        #[cfg(test)]
   482	        if let Some(hook) = test_hook.as_ref() {
   483	            hook.claimed_acquisition
   484	                .store(state.acquisition(), Ordering::SeqCst);
   485	        }
   486	        self.resize_sustain.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
   487	        Some(ResizeProposal {
   488	            epoch,
   489	            target_streams: target,
   490	            add: target > live,
   491	        })
   492	    }
   493
   494	    /// sf-2: shape-correction proposal. On push the daemon proposes the
   495	    /// epoch-0 stream count from whatever manifest prefix it has seen at
   496	    /// the early flush (`FILE_LIST_EARLY_FLUSH_ENTRIES`), so a
   497	    /// many-tiny-file push can negotiate far fewer streams than
   498	    /// [`initial_stream_proposal`] assigns the full workload. As the
   499	    /// need list accumulates client-side, the client re-runs the shape
   500	    /// table and corrects upward through the normal resize wire.
   501	    ///
   502	    /// Unlike [`Self::resize_tick`] this is a definite signal — the
   503	    /// shape is known, not inferred from throughput — so there is no
   504	    /// sustain/cooldown discipline. It still honors one-in-flight and
   505	    /// the receiver-profile ceiling, still moves ONE stream per epoch
   506	    /// (the wire carries one `sub_token` per ADD), and never proposes
   507	    /// REMOVE: shrinking below a live count is throughput evidence and
   508	    /// stays the tuner's call.
   509	    pub fn propose_shape_resize(&self, desired_streams: usize) -> Option<ResizeProposal> {
   510	        let desired = desired_streams.clamp(1, self.ceiling_max_streams.max(1));
   511	        let mut state = self
   512	            .resize_epochs
   513	            .lock()
   514	            .expect("resize epoch state poisoned");
   515	        if state.refused || state.pending_epoch.is_some() {
   516	            return None;
   517	        }
   518	        let live = self.live_streams.load(Ordering::Relaxed).max(1);
   519	        if desired <= live {
   520	            return None;
   521	        }
   522	        let epoch = state.settled_epoch.checked_add(1)?;
   523	        state.pending_epoch = Some(epoch);
   524	        Some(ResizeProposal {
   525	            epoch,
   526	            target_streams: live + 1,
   527	            add: true,
   528	        })
   529	    }
   530
   531	    /// Settle the in-flight proposal with what ACTUALLY happened:
   532	    /// `effective_streams` is the live count now in effect (from the
   533	    /// peer's ack, or the local count if a post-ack dial failed and
   534	    /// nothing changed). `accepted = false` leaves the live count
   535	    /// untouched, consumes the refused epoch, and permanently disables
   536	    /// further proposals on this transfer. Stale epochs (not the pending
   537	    /// one) are ignored. Either way the cooldown clock restarts.
   538	    pub fn resize_settled(&self, epoch: u32, effective_streams: usize, accepted: bool) {
   539	        #[cfg(test)]
   540	        let mut state = {
   541	            let hook = self
   542	                .resize_settle_test_hook
   543	                .lock()
   544	                .expect("resize settle test hook poisoned")
   545	                .clone();
   546	            if let Some(hook) = hook {
   547	                match self.resize_epochs.try_lock() {
   548	                    Ok(state) => {
   549	                        hook.contended.store(false, Ordering::SeqCst);
   550	                        hook.observed.wait();
   551	                        state
   552	                    }
   553	                    Err(std::sync::TryLockError::WouldBlock) => {
   554	                        hook.contended.store(true, Ordering::SeqCst);
   555	                        hook.observed.wait();
   556	                        self.resize_epochs
   557	                            .lock()
   558	                            .expect("resize epoch state poisoned")
   559	                    }
   560	                    Err(std::sync::TryLockError::Poisoned(_)) => {
   561	                        panic!("resize epoch state poisoned")
   562	                    }
   563	                }
   564	            } else {
   565	                self.resize_epochs
   566	                    .lock()
   567	                    .expect("resize epoch state poisoned")
   568	            }
   569	        };
   570	        #[cfg(not(test))]
   571	        let mut state = self
   572	            .resize_epochs
   573	            .lock()
   574	            .expect("resize epoch state poisoned");
   575	        self.resize_settled_locked(&mut state, epoch, effective_streams, accepted);
   576	    }
   577
   578	    fn resize_settled_locked(
   579	        &self,
   580	        state: &mut ResizeEpochState,
   581	        epoch: u32,
   582	        effective_streams: usize,
   583	        accepted: bool,
   584	    ) -> bool {
   585	        if state.pending_epoch != Some(epoch) || epoch == 0 {
   586	            return false;
   587	        }
   588	        self.ticks_since_settle.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
   589	        self.resize_sustain.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
   590	        if accepted {
   591	            let clamped = effective_streams.clamp(1, self.ceiling_max_streams.max(1));
   592	            self.live_streams.store(clamped, Ordering::Relaxed);
   593	        }
   594	        // A refused request was still an observed wire epoch. Consuming it
   595	        // keeps future/duplicate traffic monotonic even though live count
   596	        // remains unchanged.
   597	        let settled = state.settle(epoch, accepted);
   598	        debug_assert!(settled);
   599	        settled
   600	    }
   601
   602	    /// Raise max_streams toward the ceiling (used when a peer's
   603	    /// negotiation allows more than the floor; still profile-bounded).
   604	    pub fn allow_streams_up_to(&self, streams: usize) {
   605	        let clamped = streams.clamp(1, self.ceiling_max_streams.max(1));
   606	        self.max_streams.store(clamped, Ordering::Relaxed);
   607	    }
   608
   609	    // ── tuner steps ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
   610	    /// One upward step of the cheap dials: chunk ×2 toward the
  1128	            "shape correction must not retry a refused resize"
  1129	        );
  1130
  1131	        // Duplicate/stale settlements cannot reopen the terminal policy or
  1132	        // rewrite the consumed epoch/live count.
  1133	        dial.resize_settled(proposal.epoch, 5, true);
  1134	        assert_eq!(dial.resize_epoch(), proposal.epoch);
  1135	        assert_eq!(dial.live_streams(), 4);
  1136	        assert_eq!(dial.propose_shape_resize(8), None);
  1137	    }
  1138
  1139	    #[test]
  1140	    fn resize_epoch_lock_serializes_producers_across_settlement() {
  1141	        // The tuner lock spans eligibility, direction, live count, and claim:
  1142	        // pause after it acquires the lock and prove the arbitration mutex is
  1143	        // unavailable until the tick continues. This deterministically guards
  1144	        // the stale-decision window the re-review found.
  1145	        let accepted = Arc::new(TransferDial::conservative());
  1146	        while accepted.step_up_cheap_dials() {}
  1147	        burn_cooldown(&accepted);
  1148	        assert_eq!(accepted.resize_tick(1024, 0.0), None, "sustain tick 1");
  1149	        let hook = Arc::new(ResizeTickTestHook::new());
  1150	        *accepted
  1151	            .resize_tick_test_hook
  1152	            .lock()
  1153	            .expect("resize tick test hook poisoned") = Some(Arc::clone(&hook));
  1154	        let tuner = {
  1155	            let accepted = Arc::clone(&accepted);
  1156	            std::thread::spawn(move || accepted.resize_tick(1024, 0.0))
  1157	        };
  1158	        hook.entered.wait();
  1159	        let lock_spans_tick = matches!(
  1160	            accepted.resize_epochs.try_lock(),
  1161	            Err(std::sync::TryLockError::WouldBlock)
  1162	        );
  1163	        // Start the matching accepted settlement while the tuner is paused.
  1164	        // The settlement hook reports only after `try_lock` has actually
  1165	        // observed epoch arbitration, so this does not rely on a pre-call
  1166	        // channel signal or scheduler timing.
  1167	        let settle_hook = Arc::new(ResizeSettleTestHook::new());
  1168	        *accepted
  1169	            .resize_settle_test_hook
  1170	            .lock()
  1171	            .expect("resize settle test hook poisoned") = Some(Arc::clone(&settle_hook));
  1172	        let settler = {
  1173	            let accepted = Arc::clone(&accepted);
  1174	            std::thread::spawn(move || accepted.resize_settled(1, 2, true))
  1175	        };
  1176	        settle_hook.observed.wait();
  1177	        let settlement_contended = settle_hook.contended.load(Ordering::SeqCst);
  1178	        hook.release.wait();
  1179	        *accepted
  1180	            .resize_tick_test_hook
  1181	            .lock()
  1182	            .expect("resize tick test hook poisoned") = None;
  1183	        assert!(lock_spans_tick, "tuner released arbitration before claim");
  1184	        let first = tuner.join().unwrap().expect("tuner owns epoch 1");
  1185	        let entered_acquisition = hook.entered_acquisition.load(Ordering::SeqCst);
  1186	        let claimed_acquisition = hook.claimed_acquisition.load(Ordering::SeqCst);
  1187	        assert_ne!(entered_acquisition, 0, "tuner acquisition was recorded");
  1188	        assert_eq!(
  1189	            claimed_acquisition, entered_acquisition,
  1190	            "tuner released and reacquired arbitration before claim"
  1191	        );
  1192	        assert_eq!(first.epoch, 1);
  1193	        settler.join().unwrap();
  1194	        *accepted
  1195	            .resize_settle_test_hook
  1196	            .lock()
  1197	            .expect("resize settle test hook poisoned") = None;
  1198	        assert!(
  1199	            settlement_contended,
  1200	            "accepted settlement reached arbitration without contention"
  1201	        );
  1202	        assert_eq!(accepted.live_streams(), 2, "accepted settlement applied");
  1203	        assert!(!accepted.resize_pending(), "accepted epoch settled");
  1204	        assert_eq!(
  1205	            accepted.resize_tick(1024, 0.0),
  1206	            None,
  1207	            "accepted settlement resets cooldown"
  1208	        );
  1209
  1210	        // Refusal crossing: a shape producer begins while the settlement
  1211	        // lock is held. Once the same production helper records refusal and
  1212	        // releases the lock, the waiter must observe terminal state and may
  1213	        // not claim epoch 2.
  1214	        let refused = Arc::new(TransferDial::conservative());
  1215	        let first = refused.propose_shape_resize(8).expect("shape owns epoch 1");
  1216	        let mut refused_state = refused
  1217	            .resize_epochs
  1218	            .lock()
  1219	            .expect("resize epoch state poisoned");
  1220	        let (started_tx, started_rx) = std::sync::mpsc::channel();
  1221	        let shape = {
  1222	            let refused = Arc::clone(&refused);
  1223	            std::thread::spawn(move || {
  1224	                started_tx.send(()).unwrap();
  1225	                refused.propose_shape_resize(8)
  1226	            })
  1227	        };
  1228	        started_rx.recv().unwrap();
  1229	        assert!(refused.resize_settled_locked(&mut refused_state, first.epoch, 1, false));
  1230	        drop(refused_state);
  1231	        assert_eq!(shape.join().unwrap(), None, "refusal is terminal");
  1232	        assert_eq!(refused.resize_epoch(), first.epoch);
  1233	        assert!(!refused.resize_pending());
  1234	    }
  1235
  1236	    #[test]
  1237	    fn resize_target_clamps_to_the_profile_ceiling() {
  1238	        let dial = TransferDial::conservative_within(Some(&profile(4, 0, 0)));
  1239	        dial.set_negotiated_streams(4); // already at the profile ceiling
  1240	        while dial.step_up_cheap_dials() {}

    14	You are being asked whether an experiment is worth running, and whether it will answer the question it claims to answer. Do NOT assume the plan is right. The plan is mine, it has been rewritten eleven times, and every round has found something wrong with it — including the plan itself. Argue with it.
    15
    16	THE SITUATION
    17
    18	A file-transfer tool (blit) has one performance finding standing between it and release. Call it P1:
    19
    20	  On a macOS <-> Windows pair, over TCP, on a "mixed" fixture (5001 files, 547MB),
    21	  the transfer is ~25-38% slower when the DESTINATION machine initiates it than when
    22	  the SOURCE machine does. Same code, same bytes, same direction of data flow — only
    23	  which end typed the command changes. Measured four times (ratios 1.237, 1.300,
    24	  1.385, 1.362). The project's acceptance criterion says no transfer may vary by more
    25	  than 10% based on who initiated it, so P1 blocks the release.
    26
    27	What is known:
    28	  - Linux <-> Linux: NO P1 (8/8 cells pass, the same cell reads 1.092/1.003).
    29	  - macOS <-> Windows: P1, every time.
    30	  - MTU is NOT the cause (a 256-run A-B-B-A experiment at 9000 vs 1500 killed it: r = -3.1%).
    31	  - macOS <-> macOS: NEVER TESTED. That is the missing cell of the 2x2.
    32	  - A code-level hypothesis (H1) accuses blit's own dial/accept paths — which run on macOS too.
    33
    34	THE PROPOSED EXPERIMENT (the thing you are reviewing)
    35
    36	Run the destination-initiated vs source-initiated comparison on a pair of Macs (nagatha
    37	and q, 10GbE, MTU 9000), to answer ONE question: can P1 occur WITHOUT a Windows peer?
    38
    39	  - 6 cells: 2 measurands (TCP x mixed, both directions) + 4 controls (gRPC x mixed both
    40	    directions, TCP x large-file both directions). The controls exist because P1 is claimed
    41	    to be specific to TCP x mixed: if the controls show the same asymmetry, the rig itself is
    42	    biased and nothing can be read off it.
    43	  - 8 ABBA pairs per cell, slot-major (every cell takes one pair before any cell takes its
    44	    next), so all cells see the same wall-clock window and the same transients.
    45	  - A pre-registered decision rule, computed by the harness, so no one can look at the numbers
    46	    and then invent a favourable reading.
    47
    48	The files:
    49	  docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md   the design + the decision rule
    50	  scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh                            the harness (gates, ABBA, timing)
    51	  scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py                          the decision rule, mechanized
    52	  scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py                     its guard test
    53	  docs/plan/OTP12_PERF_FINDINGS.md                        the parent plan (what P1/P2 are)
    54
    55	NO DATA HAS EVER BEEN TAKEN. The instrument has been reviewed eleven times, ~110 defects
    56	found and fixed, and it has still never run. That is itself a signal I want you to weigh.
    57
    58	WHAT I ACTUALLY WANT FROM YOU — in this order:
    59
    60	1. WILL THIS EXPERIMENT ANSWER THE QUESTION? Take the goal seriously and forget my design for
    61	   a moment. Given what is known (the 2x2 above, MTU killed, H1 accusing blit's own code), is
    62	   a Mac<->Mac reproduction run the right next move at all? What would you do instead, or
    63	   first? Consider cheaper, more direct, more diagnostic alternatives — e.g. instrumenting the
    64	   dial/accept path directly, packet capture, a targeted micro-benchmark of the suspected code
    65	   path, or bisecting the code hypothesis. If the experiment is worth running, say so. If it
    66	   is a detour that will produce an ambiguous result whatever happens, say THAT — plainly.
    67
    68	2. WHAT DOES EACH POSSIBLE OUTCOME ACTUALLY BUY? The rig can say REPRODUCES, DOES-NOT-
    69	   REPRODUCE, MIXED, UNCLEAR, CONTROLS-NOT-CLEAN, INCOMPLETE, RIG-VOID. For each, what does
    70	   the project legitimately learn, and what would we be tempted to conclude that we may NOT?
    71	   Is there an outcome that would change what we do next? If NO outcome changes the next
    72	   action, the experiment is theatre and you should say so.
    73
    74	3. IS THE DESIGN SOUND, and is it the right SHAPE? Are the controls the right controls? Is
    75	   ABBA + slot-major the right schedule? Is n=8 pairs per cell enough to see a 25-38% effect,
    76	   and is it enough to support the NULL the rule can emit? (The rule judges a null on the full
    77	   range of all 8 paired differences.) Is anything important missing — a confound not
    78	   controlled, a cell not run, an alternative explanation the design cannot separate?
    79
    80	4. IS THE DECISION RULE THE RIGHT RULE? It is deliberately austere: one threshold
    81	   T = min(10% of the source arm, 230ms), four cell states from an exact order-statistic CI,
    82	   controls must be clean at T/2, and a bias term B (what the clean controls could not exclude)
    83	   that must only ever HARDEN a verdict. Is that sound? Is it too clever? Would a
    84	   statistician recognize it, and would they trust a null out of it? Note n is EXACTLY 8, so
    85	   the >=95% CI IS [min,max].
    86
    87	5. ONLY THEN: correctness bugs. Anything in the harness or the rule that would produce a WRONG
    88	   measurement or a WRONG verdict. Two defect classes recur in every round and you should
    89	   assume both are still present: (a) a fix applied where it was SHOWN rather than where the
    90	   defect LIVED (the duplicate site is left broken), and (b) a protection that never actually
    91	   executes, or that cannot fail — e.g. an equal-settle window that was dead for three
    92	   revisions while the document asserted it, and, this round, pinned constants placed above
    93	   the check meant to enforce them so the harness refused every run.
    94
    95	You may run `SELFTEST=1 bash scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh` (exercises the gates on both Macs,
    96	takes NO data), and `python3 scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py [--mutations]`.
    97
    98	Be direct. If the honest answer is "this is a well-built instrument pointed at the wrong

   137	The schedule is more careful than the causal question warrants, but I’ve found a structural issue already: the end-of-session “fabric re-check” verifies only MSS and source IP, not negotiated link speed.    174	codex
   175	The Mac↔Mac run is not the right next move. At best, a positive result gives you a convenient local reproduction rig. It does not adjudicate H1, identify a cause, or alter P1’s release obligation. A negative result is especially weak because the Mac↔Mac harness introduces the corrected equal-settle method that was not used for the Mac↔Windows measurements defining P1.
   176
   177	First, rerun the known-failing Mac↔Windows cell with this corrected timing method while recording dial, accept, ACK, resize-epoch, first-byte, and completion timestamps; take a packet capture concurrently. If P1 survives, test H1 with a causal dial/accept counterfactual. A targeted microbenchmark comes after the trace identifies a phase, because the mixed-only interaction may disappear in isolation. A cheap two-cell Mac↔Mac screen could then establish whether local debugging is possible; the current 96-transfer apparatus is disproportionate.
   178
   179	## What each outcome buys
   180
   181	| Outcome | Legitimate learning | Not licensed |
   182	|---|---|---|
   183	| `REPRODUCES` | P1-shaped behavior can occur on these Macs without Windows; use them as a local debugging rig. | H1 confirmed or killed; platform-general behavior; Windows exonerated as a contributor; release acceptance. |
   184	| `DOES-NOT-REPRODUCE` | Under this method and window, both directions exclude a median gap of the registered size, subject to the statistical assumptions. | Windows is necessary; H1 confirmed; the four Mac↔Windows observations retracted; no tail or intermittent effect. |
   185	| `MIXED` | Strong host×role, ordering, or endpoint interaction requiring decomposition. | Either reproduction or null. |
   186	| `INVERTED` | A new, opposite initiator asymmetry. | P1 absent. |
   187	| `UNCLEAR` | The rig did not resolve the margin. | A weak null or partial reproduction. |
   188	| `CONTROLS-NOT-CLEAN` | Either a control has initiator asymmetry or the controls cannot bound the proposed bias. | Anything about P1 itself. |
   189	| `INCOMPLETE` | Only an execution/feasibility failure. | Any performance inference. |
   190	| `RIG-VOID` | Environmental conditions invalidated the session. | Any performance inference. |
   191
   192	Only `REPRODUCES` changes anything operationally: it changes where debugging can happen. Every substantive path still leads to direct instrumentation and a causal counterfactual, so the full experiment is close to theatre even before its remaining defects.
   193
   194	## (3) Design findings
   195
   196	- **BLOCKER — no same-method positive control.** The new instrument deliberately inserts and verifies a settle before fsync at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:925](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:925), because the previous timing gap could manufacture a one-directional effect. The original rig-W harness flushes immediately at [bench_otp12_win.sh:505](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12_win.sh:505). Before asking whether Windows is necessary, establish that P1 still exists on Mac↔Windows under the corrected method. Otherwise a Mac↔Mac null conflates platform pairing with measurement repair.
   197
   198	- **HIGH — the controls do not fully support “TCP × mixed-specific.”** The registered controls are gRPC-mixed and TCP-large at [PREREGISTRATION.md:164](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:164), but prior evidence defining the signature also uses TCP-small as a clean fixture control at [OTP12_PERF_FINDINGS.md:228](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/plan/OTP12_PERF_FINDINGS.md:228). Mixed contains 5,000 small files; TCP-small is the most relevant check for a small-file/metadata interaction masquerading as “mixed-only.”
   199
   200	- **HIGH — n=8 is enough for an exact median CI, not enough to promise a useful answer.** Under independent, identically distributed paired differences, `[min,max]` has 99.22% coverage for the population median. But `EFFECT` requires every pair to clear the margin and `NONE` requires every pair inside it. One soft pair produces `UNCLEAR`; modest control noise produces `CONTROLS-NOT-CLEAN`. No empirical power calculation justifies expecting decisiveness. ABBA controls first-order drift but does not establish independence or stationarity. See [PREREGISTRATION.md:201](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:201) and [PREREGISTRATION.md:380](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:380).
   201
   202	- **HIGH — the null’s estimand is unclear.** If the claim is median equivalence, the exact CI is recognizable and defensible under IID assumptions. If “no transfer may vary by more than 10%” means individual or future transfers, eight observed values inside the margin cannot establish that; it is not a population tolerance bound. The document alternates between median inference and “every pair” rhetoric at [PREREGISTRATION.md:250](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:250).
   203
   204	- **MEDIUM — in-place retries weaken contemporaneous controls.** A voided cell retries repeatedly before its siblings run at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1118](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1118). After several long retries, its valid observation and the controls no longer share the same transient. Retry after completing the rest of the slot, or explicitly abandon that slot.
   205
   206	AB/BA alternation, slot-major traversal, and rotation are otherwise the right general scheduling shape.
   207
   208	## (4) Decision-rule findings
   209
   210	- **BLOCKER — `min()` implements the opposite of the stated policy.** The document says an effect must matter by both the 10% bar and the 230 ms reference at [PREREGISTRATION.md:212](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:212). `min(src/10, 230)` lets either smaller standard win. The test deliberately labels `230 ms @ 2500 ms` as `REPRODUCES` at [otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:111](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:111), even though its ratio is 1.092 and the engine’s own acceptance bar says `PASS`. Thus the engine can confidently report “P1 reproduces” when P1’s defining invariance criterion passes.
   211
   212	- **BLOCKER — the exact 10% boundary is simultaneously PASS and REPRODUCES.** The bar treats `≤1.10` as PASS at [otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:282](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:282), while `classify()` uses `CI_lo >= T` at [otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:251](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:251). The guard explicitly requires an exact 10% effect to reproduce at [otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:217](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:217). That is a direct policy contradiction, not statistical conservatism.
   213
   214	- **HIGH — `B` is not a statistically justified bias bound.** It assumes rig bias scales multiplicatively with arm time at [otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:303](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:303). The known SSH dispatch residual is naturally additive in milliseconds, while free-writeback response may be nonlinear. A 49 ms additive bias observed on a slow control is scaled downward on a faster measurand and can under-harden a verdict. No control evidence establishes the chosen proportional model. A conservative bound would need a predeclared confound model—at minimum the maximum of additive and proportional bounds—or closely speed-matched controls.
   215
   216	- **HIGH — the `B ≥ T/2` refusal checks only the positive margin.** The implementation checks `t_pos` at [otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:343](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:343), while the actual negative margin is smaller (`src/11`) at [otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:220](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:220). `B` can therefore exceed half the relevant inverted/null margin while the session still grades.
   217
   218	- **MEDIUM — the preregistration does not register one unambiguous rule.** Its summary describes a single symmetric `±T` at [PREREGISTRATION.md:7](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:7), while the body and engine use separate `src/10` and `src/11` margins at [PREREGISTRATION.md:214](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:214). That matters to `INVERTED`, `NONE`, control certification, and the bias refusal.
   219
   220	The four implemented cell states are otherwise mutually exclusive and exhaustive for valid positive timings. Exact n=8 enforcement, per-cell B hardening, and MIXED-on-unhardened-states look internally correct. A statistician would recognize the order-statistic CI; they would not recognize the B construction and session policy as a calibrated inferential procedure.
   221
   222	## (5) Correctness bugs
   223
   224	- **BLOCKER — the end-fabric protection does not recheck the full fabric it promises.** Startup checks MTU, negotiated `10Gbase-T`, and link status at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:499](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:499). The end gate checks only MSS and local IP at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:557](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:557). A link can renegotiate from 10GbE to 1GbE while retaining MTU 9000, MSS 8948, and the same IP; the session will grade. This is exactly the duplicate-site defect class.
   225
   226	- **HIGH — failed landed-tree cleanup records a marker but does not invalidate anything.** `clear_landed()` touches `LANDED-TREE-SURVIVED` at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1041](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1041), but the marker is never consulted before verdict computation at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1354](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1354). Repeated survivors can change disk occupancy and later I/O while the session still reports a confident verdict.
   227
   228	- **MEDIUM — manifest fabric/hash probes discard producer status.** Local verdict/test hashes are not validated at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:739](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:739), and `mss_read` is embedded inside `echo` at [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:750](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:750). A failed reader produces an empty “measured fabric” entry while `echo` succeeds and the run continues.
   229
   230	- **MEDIUM — mutation “kills” can occur for the wrong reason.** The mutation runner counts any unexpected outcome—including a crash or a different safe refusal—as a kill at [otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:537](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:537). In the run, the missing-cell mutation was “killed” by `ENGINE-CRASH`, and the zero-time mutation by `CONTROLS-NOT-CLEAN`, rather than exercising their claimed dangerous verdicts. The suite detects edits, but some mutations do not prove the safety property attributed to them.
   231
   232	Validation completed: the verdict suite reported 40/40 cases, 19/19 mutations, and 300/300 fuzz inputs. The harness selftest completed with zero blind gates; quiescence correctly fired because `codex` was running. It took no transfer data. As the selftest itself states, it does not exercise daemon lifecycle, smoke transfer, ABBA execution, pair voiding, or the manifest.
   233
   234	**DO NOT RUN IT — first validate P1 with the corrected measurement on the known-failing Mac↔Windows pair and instrument the suspected path; the current rule can call a 1.092/PASS cell “REPRODUCES,” and the end-fabric gate is incomplete.**

    14	A benchmark harness on my own two Macs is about to take its first data. Before it does, I want to know whether the numbers it produces will be TRUE. Read scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh (and docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md for what it is meant to do).
    15
    16	What it does: runs file transfers between two Macs I own, times them, records the numbers. It uses ssh to reach the second machine, sudo to drop the page cache between runs so every run reads cold, and pgrep/iostat/top/ifconfig to confirm both machines are idle and on the right network before it measures anything. Those are the measurement mechanics. The review is about whether they are CORRECT.
    17
    18	WHAT IS AT STAKE: the numbers decide whether a performance finding — destination-initiated transfers paying 25-38% more than source-initiated ones — reproduces on a pair of Macs, which in turn shapes a release decision. A wrong number is worse than no number, because it will be believed. This project has ALREADY retracted three claims that turned out to be harness bugs rather than real findings.
    19
    20	DO NOT limit yourself to checking a list of fixes. The most valuable thing you can tell me is something I have not thought of. Specifically:
    21
    22	1. WOULD YOU TRUST A NUMBER OUT OF THIS? Read it as an experimentalist, not a linter. Is the
    23	   timing methodology sound? The measurand is wall time of one transfer, timed by the
    24	   INITIATING host in a single process that also spawns the client (because cross-process
    25	   time.monotonic() on macOS is process-relative and produced garbage — that bug survived two
    26	   rounds). Per timed run it: purges both page caches, waits for the destination disk to go
    27	   quiet, runs the transfer, then walks the landed tree calling fsync on every file and ADDS
    28	   that flush time to the measurement. Is adding the fsync walk to wall time defensible, or is
    29	   it measuring something other than what we claim? Is there an asymmetry between the two arms
    30	   (one arm runs the client locally, the other runs it over ssh on the far machine) that the
    31	   design does not neutralize? What confound would you expect to bite FIRST?
    32
    33	2. WHAT WOULD MAKE THIS RIG LIE? Name the most plausible way this harness produces a
    34	   confident, wrong, one-directional result — one that looks exactly like the effect we are
    35	   hunting. Free page-cache writeback between the client exiting and the fsync starting is
    36	   the one I know about (it is longer for whichever arm ran over ssh, so it REVERSES SIGN WITH
    37	   DIRECTION — it can manufacture the finding out of nothing). It is supposedly neutralized by
    38	   an equal 250ms settle on both arms, which is now measured and voids the pair if it did not
    39	   elapse. Is that actually sufficient? What else is direction-asymmetric here?
    40
    41	3. CORRECTNESS DEFECTS. Two classes recur in EVERY round; assume both are still present:
    42	   (a) THE FIX WAS APPLIED WHERE IT WAS SHOWN, NOT WHERE IT LIVED. A fail-open pgrep was fixed
    43	       in one gate and left identical in its duplicate. The drain was fixed by validating its
    44	       VALUE and left failing by discarding its EXIT STATUS — and a round later, three MORE
    45	       substitutions in that same function were still discarding theirs. Find the next duplicate.
    46	   (b) A PROTECTION THAT NEVER EXECUTES, OR CANNOT FAIL, OR CANNOT PASS. The equal settle had
    47	       NEVER RUN for three revisions (an awk quoting bug killed the sleep and its status was
    48	       discarded) while the document asserted it. `bash -n` sees nothing. Writing THIS revision
    49	       produced two more of the same class, both caught only by RUNNING it: pinned constants
    50	       placed above the check meant to enforce them, so the harness refused every run; and a
    51	       sentinel frame delimited with ':' wrapped around a MAC address, which is all colons, so
    52	       a gate went blind on a good link.
    53	   Concretely: every exit status a later command can discard (pipelines take the LAST command's
    54	   status; command substitutions; `|| true`; values validated without checking that the probe
    55	   producing them succeeded). Every gate that reads a number — can an error, an empty string or
    56	   a non-number read as PASSING? Every sentinel frame — can the framed value contain the
    57	   delimiter? `set -euo pipefail` interactions, and functions that set a global while being
    58	   called inside `$(...)` (that has bitten twice). macOS ships bash 3.2: no associative arrays,
    59	   no mapfile.
    60
    61	4. THE SCHEDULER: run_all_cells/run_one_pair. 6 cells x 8 ABBA pairs, slot-major with the cell
    62	   order rotating per slot. Void/retry accounting, arm alternation, CSV integrity, and whether a
    63	   cell exhausting its attempts can corrupt another cell's data.
    64
    65	5. DOES THE HARNESS DO WHAT THE PRE-REGISTRATION SAYS — and, more importantly, does the
    66	   pre-registration describe something worth doing? Any behaviour in the code that the document
    67	   does not register is a way to tune the rig after seeing the data, which is the one thing this
    68	   whole apparatus exists to prevent.
    69
    70	Run it yourself: `SELFTEST=1 bash scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh` exercises every gate on both
    71	Macs and takes NO data. Reading its output is worth more than reading the script.
    72
    73	Output a concise markdown findings list (file:line, severity BLOCKER/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW, rationale),
    74	led by a short prose answer to (1) and (2). Then a final VERDICT line: READY or NOT READY.

   135	No. I trust the single-process client timer itself; the self-test measured a 1 s sleep as 1010/1012 ms. I do not trust the reported `ms` or resulting verdict yet. It is not transfer wall time: it is client wall time plus a delayed tree-walk/fsync interval, excluding the intervening 250 ms. That is a synthetic metric, and the walk does not establish whole-tree durability because directories are never synced.
   136
   137	The most plausible directional lie is the fully confounded execution context: nagatha’s client always runs locally; q’s always runs beneath `sshd`. Host performance and local-vs-SSH scheduling therefore reverse with direction exactly like the sought effect. The equal settle also does not equalize client-exit→fsync-start; it merely adds 250 ms after the unequal gap.
   138
   139	The [self-test log](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/logs/otp12pf_mac_20260714T181745/bench.log) passed 40/40 cases and 19/19 mutations with zero blind gates; quiescence correctly fired on this Codex session. It did not exercise daemon lifecycle, transfers, scheduling, pair voiding, or the manifest.

   141	Findings:
   142
   143	- [PREREGISTRATION.md:214](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:214), [otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:220](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict.py:220), [otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:111](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/otp12pf_mac_verdict_test.py:111) — **BLOCKER** — `min(10%, 230 ms)` implements “either,” not the documented “both.” The guard intentionally calls 230 ms on a 2500 ms arm `REPRODUCES` although ratio 1.092 passes the defining invariance bar. Exact 1.10 is likewise both `PASS` and `REPRODUCES`.
   144
   145	- [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:925](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:925), [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1007](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1007) — **BLOCKER** — `ms = client wall + post-settle walk/fsync`; it excludes settle and includes enumeration/open overhead. It is neither continuous transfer wall time nor a complete durability latency. Relabel and report separately, or time continuously to a defined durability barrier.
   146
   147	- [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:257](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:257), [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:976](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:976) — **BLOCKER** — nagatha initiators run locally while q initiators run under SSH. Host and launch context are perfectly collinear with arm and reverse with direction. Run both through identical execution contexts, such as SSH to both hosts.
   148
   149	- [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:958](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:958), [bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1009](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1009) — **BLOCKER** — the 250 ms sleep starts after the unequal return/redispatch gap. The harness accepts RTT up to 62 ms, unrelated to `T` and absent from `B`; that can approach the entire threshold on a fast arm. Measuring only the sleep proves the sleep, not equalization.

   165	VERDICT: NOT READY
   166	tokens used
   167	258,492
   168	No. I trust the single-process client timer itself; the self-test measured a 1 s sleep as 1010/1012 ms. I do not trust the reported `ms` or resulting verdict yet. It is not transfer wall time: it is client wall time plus a delayed tree-walk/fsync interval, excluding the intervening 250 ms. That is a synthetic metric, and the walk does not establish whole-tree durability because directories are never synced.
   169
   170	The most plausible directional lie is the fully confounded execution context: nagatha’s client always runs locally; q’s always runs beneath `sshd`. Host performance and local-vs-SSH scheduling therefore reverse with direction exactly like the sought effect. The equal settle also does not equalize client-exit→fsync-start; it merely adds 250 ms after the unequal gap.
   171
   172	The [self-test log](/Users/michael/Dev/blit_v2/logs/otp12pf_mac_20260714T181745/bench.log) passed 40/40 cases and 19/19 mutations with zero blind gates; quiescence correctly fired on this Codex session. It did not exercise daemon lifecycle, transfers, scheduling, pair voiding, or the manifest.
   173

Exact 1.10 is likewise both `PASS` and `REPRODUCES`.

scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:5:# This revision fixes round 11: the BLOCKER (the registered topology was NOT
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:6:# enforced — NIC/IP/MAC were env-overridable and the MTU and link speed were never
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:7:# checked AT ALL, so the run could go over the 1GbE NIC or at MTU 1500, after pf-0
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:12:# the round-11 topology fix immediately produced another: the pinned literals were
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:76:# TOPOLOGY: the driver runs on nagatha; the nagatha end is LOCAL and q is over
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:114:# link speed were NEVER CHECKED AT ALL. So the whole run could go over the 1GbE NIC, or at
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:116:# MTU moves wall time on this fabric. THE RIG MUST PROVE IT IS ON THE FABRIC IT CLAIMS.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:120:# The identity is pinned here; topology_gate() and mss_gate() below PROVE it on the wire.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:126:# --- nagatha: LOCAL end (driver) ---------------------------------------------
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:132:# --- q: REMOTE end ------------------------------------------------------------

scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:411:  [[ "$running" -eq 0 ]] || die "$(hname "$h"): a Time Machine backup is RUNNING — it hammers CPU and disk on a bench end (one destination is on skippy, the same 10GbE fabric)."
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:451:  # correct check: a stale entry on the 1GbE NIC is irrelevant to the 10GbE path.)
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:468:    || die "$(hname "$h"): no ARP entry for $peer_ip ON $nic — the 10GbE path has not resolved the peer"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:479:    || die "$(hname "$h"): route to $peer_ip egresses '$route_nic', not the 10GbE NIC '$(hnic "$h")' — the multi-NIC trap (macOS routes by network SERVICE order, so a 1GbE NIC can win and every run would go over gigabit)."
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:485:# says NOTHING about the NIC's SPEED or the path's MTU: a 1GbE interface, or a 9000-MTU NIC
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:487:# wall time on this fabric, so an unverified MTU is not a footnote -- it is a confound sitting
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:491:#   topology_gate  the LOCAL NIC: its MTU, its NEGOTIATED media (a 1GbE link says 1000baseT),
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:493:#   mss_gate       the PATH, end to end: a real TCP connection to the peer's registered IP,
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:494:#                  and the MSS the two kernels agreed on. Both ends can sit at MTU 9000 while
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:497:#                  traffic EGRESSED the registered 10GbE IP and not one of the eight other
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:499:topology_gate() {
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:513:    || die "$(hname "$h"): $nic is at MTU $mtu, but the REGISTERED fabric is MTU $REGISTERED_MTU. pf-0 spent 256 runs establishing that MTU moves wall time on this rig — a run at the wrong MTU is not the registered experiment."
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:515:    || die_blind "$(hname "$h"): $nic reports no negotiated media — refusing (an unknown link speed is not a 10GbE link)"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:520:  log "  fabric on $(hname "$h"): $nic mtu $mtu, media $media, status $st"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:526:# AND at the end of the session (VOID, the data exists) — pf-0's start-and-end rule — so the
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:532:  # Port 22 is sshd, up on both ends by definition (the harness itself got here over it).
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:554:  log "  path from $(hname "$h") -> $(hname "$(other "$h")") ($peer): MSS $mss via $local_ip — the fabric is jumbo end-to-end"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:557:end_mss_gate() {   # SESSION-END policy: the data exists, so VOID it rather than die.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:562:      SESSION_VOID_REASON="the path MSS on $(hname "$h") could not be read at the END of the session — a session whose fabric cannot be confirmed at both ends cannot be graded"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:567:      SESSION_VOID_REASON="the fabric CHANGED under the session: $(hname "$h") now negotiates MSS $mss via $local_ip (registered: $REGISTERED_MSS via $(hip "$h")). A link that flapped or re-routed mid-session may have carried some of the timed windows"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:658:    ts.append((time.monotonic() - t) * 1000.0)
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:722:    topology_gate "$h"          # the NIC: MTU + negotiated media (never checked before r11)
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:747:    # session ran at; a session that does not record its own fabric cannot be re-read later.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:749:    echo "# fabric_registered: nic_mtu=$REGISTERED_MTU media=$REGISTERED_MEDIA mss=$REGISTERED_MSS"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:750:    echo "# fabric_measured: nagatha=[$(mss_read n)] q=[$(mss_read q)]  (mss local_ip)"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:895:  # Purge BOTH ends first — the purge itself dirties the disk, so a drain certified
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:968:    ts.append((time.monotonic() - t) * 1000.0)
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1141:# The end-load is a CONDITION OF THE SESSION, not a log line. A mid-session load
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1143:# it could not void anything: the code logged `load1 (end)` and computed a verdict
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1147:end_load_gate() {
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1152:      SESSION_VOID_REASON="end-load on $(hname "$h") could not be read (got '$l') — a session whose end conditions are unknown cannot be graded"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1157:      SESSION_VOID_REASON="end-load on $(hname "$h") is $l (> $LOAD_MAX) — the machine was NOT quiet at the end of the session, so a contaminant may have entered the timed windows"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1180:# A gate can end in three states, and the old self-test collapsed two of them
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1203:  # verified. errexit is suspended in THIS shell only, and re-armed INSIDE the subshell.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1271:    gate_probe "link          (ARP on the egress NIC + 10GbE route)" link_gate "$h"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1272:    gate_probe "fabric NIC    (MTU $REGISTERED_MTU + negotiated $REGISTERED_MEDIA)" topology_gate "$h"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1273:    gate_probe "fabric PATH   (negotiated MSS $REGISTERED_MSS, egress on the registered IP)" mss_gate "$h"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1299:  SESSION_VOID_REASON=""; end_load_gate
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1301:    log "  [OK]     end-load gate (both Macs under $LOAD_MAX; it CAN void a session)"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1303:    # An UNREADABLE end-load is a blind probe, not a busy machine (grok r6, F7).
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1304:    log "  [BROKEN] end-load gate — $SESSION_VOID_REASON"; SELFTEST_BROKEN=$((SELFTEST_BROKEN+1))
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1306:    log "  [FIRED]  end-load gate — $SESSION_VOID_REASON"; SELFTEST_FIRED=$((SELFTEST_FIRED+1))
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1308:  # The END-of-session fabric re-check, exercised for real. Same three-way scoring: a probe
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1309:  # that cannot answer is BLIND, a fabric that genuinely changed is the gate WORKING.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1310:  SESSION_VOID_REASON=""; end_mss_gate
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1312:    log "  [OK]     end-fabric gate (MSS $REGISTERED_MSS on both ends; it CAN void a session)"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1314:    log "  [BROKEN] end-fabric gate — $SESSION_VOID_REASON"; SELFTEST_BROKEN=$((SELFTEST_BROKEN+1))
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1316:    log "  [FIRED]  end-fabric gate — $SESSION_VOID_REASON"; SELFTEST_FIRED=$((SELFTEST_FIRED+1))
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1331:  log "SELFTEST PASSED: every gate exercised here can answer."
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1356:  # End-load AND end-fabric, BEFORE the verdict is computed. Both can VOID the session.
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1357:  # The fabric is re-proved at the END for the same reason pf-0 did it: a link that flapped
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1358:  # or re-routed mid-session (onto the 1GbE NIC, or a 1500 hop) would have carried some of
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1361:  log "  load1 (end): nagatha=$(load1 n)  q=$(load1 q)"
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1362:  end_load_gate
scripts/bench_otp12pf_mac.sh:1363:  [[ -n "$SESSION_VOID_REASON" ]] || end_mss_gate

docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:94:to change a pre-registered rule, and every amendment is forced by a reviewer's
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:131:| **P1 REPRODUCES** | P1 **does not require a Windows peer** (on this pair), so it is **not** waivable as "Windows residue", and every code-level hypothesis strengthens. **Limits**: it does **not** establish a platform-*general* cost (two Macs are not "all platforms"); it does **not** name the mechanism; it does **not** kill H1 (the code H1 accuses runs here too); and it leaves **macOS/APFS** and **host×role** explanations fully **OPEN** — "not Windows-specific" is not "not platform-specific" (round-3 BLOCKER). |
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:142:table: **P1 is fixed to ≤1.10, or the owner amends acceptance criterion 1.**

docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:499:end-load. **A self-test that overstates itself is the very fail-open it exists to

docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:519:  refuses; an **end** `load1` above 3.0 **VOIDS THE SESSION** (`RIG-VOID`), because a
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:521:  *(Round-4, grok: rev 4 moved the end-load logging before the verdict and its
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:525:- **COLD CACHES** both ends every run (`sudo -n /usr/sbin/purge`); a failed purge
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:535:- **FIXTURES** verified by **count AND byte sum** on both ends before any timed run.
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:549:  right interface), and the route egresses the 10GbE NIC (macOS routes by service
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:550:  order, so a 1GbE NIC can win and every run would go over gigabit).
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:552:  Until rev 11 the topology was **not enforced at all**: NIC/IP/MAC were env-overridable and
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:553:  **the MTU and link speed were never checked**, so the whole run could have gone over the
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:554:  **1GbE** NIC or at **MTU 1500** — after pf-0 spent **256 runs** establishing that MTU moves
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:555:  wall time on this fabric. nagatha alone has **eight** other 1500-MTU interfaces. Now:
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:556:  * the topology (`N_IP`/`N_NIC`/`N_MAC`/`Q_IP`/`Q_NIC`/`Q_MAC`/`Q_SSH`, plus `RUNS` and
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:559:  * **the NIC**: MTU **9000**, negotiated media **10Gbase-T** (a 1GbE link reports
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:565:    chose**, which proves the traffic **egressed the registered 10GbE IP** and not one of the
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:567:  * **start AND end.** The fabric is re-proved when the session finishes; a link that flapped
docs/bench/otp12-macmac-2026-07-14/PREREGISTRATION.md:570:  * the **measured** fabric is written into the manifest, not just the registered one.

.review/results/macmac-r12.codex-design.md:37:and q, 10GbE, MTU 9000), to answer ONE question: can P1 occur WITHOUT a Windows peer?
